title: Second team as data + CLI wiring + docs (the platform proof)
description: Prove the abstraction end-to-end: define a minimal NON-medical example team purely as data (a `teams` config entry, no new code path), wire team selection into `bober run` (--team <id>) and `bober chat` (team selection), and document how to add a team. Running the example team must route to its own memory namespace, pipeline shape, and provider routing; omitting the flag uses the programming team unchanged. This is the sprint that demonstrates 'adding a team is data, not code'.

successCriteria.description: The project builds successfully with zero TypeScript errors (npm run build).
TypeScript compilation produces zero type errors in strict mode (npm run typecheck).
All tests pass with zero failures, including new CLI/team-routing tests (npm run test).

A minimal example team is defined purely as a `teams` config entry (a fixture/config, no new code branch). A unit test asserts loadTeam(config, 'example') returns its declared memoryNamespace, pipelineShape, and merged providers.

`bober run --team <id>` is additive and threads the team id to runPipeline; existing `bober run` invocations without --team behave exactly as before (programming team). A unit test asserts the flag is parsed and propagated, and that its absence yields the programming default.

`bober chat` selects a team (positional arg or --team) and routes the session's memory distill + spawned runs to that team; omitting it uses programming. A unit test asserts the chat session resolves the named team and its namespace.

End-to-end routing: a lesson produced while the example team is active lands in the example team's .bober/memory/<namespace>/ (not the programming path). A unit/integration test asserts the lesson location by namespace.

Documentation explains 'adding a team is data, not code', including the `teams` config shape, the three axes (providers/memory namespace/pipeline shape), and the deferred file-registry path. A doc file (README section or a docs/ page) is added/updated.

nonGoals: Do not build the medical team or any guardrail enforcement (Phase 6)
Do not add a .bober/teams/*.json file registry (Option A only; mention as deferred in docs)
Do not introduce semantic_facts or memory hygiene (Phase 3)
Do not add a second PRODUCTION team — the example team is a minimal validation fixture
stopConditions: `bober run --team example` and `bober chat` with the example team route to its namespace + shape + providers; default (no flag) is the programming team unchanged; docs updated
definitionOfDone: A minimal non-medical example team exists purely as data. `bober run` gains an additive --team <id> flag threaded to runPipeline (default programming); `bober chat` selects a team (positional/--team) and routes its memory distill and spawned runs to that team. A lesson produced under the example team lands in its namespace. Documentation describes how to add a team (config shape, the three axes, deferred file-registry). Build, typecheck, and all tests pass; existing no-flag behavior is unchanged.
assumptions: The example team is a small, harmless fixture (e.g. a 'docs'/'research-only' team) used to validate the abstraction; it does not need a bespoke pipeline engine — reusing 'ts' with a distinct namespace and a provider override is sufficient proof.
Phase 1's `bober chat <team>` argument was accepted but ignored (label-only); this sprint makes it actually select the team.
--team on `bober run` mirrors the additive pattern used for --run-id in Phase 1 (src/cli/commands/run.ts): absent = today's behavior.
generatorNotes:
This sprint wires together S1-S3 and proves the claim; it should add almost no new abstraction — only CLI flags, an example team fixture, routing, and docs.

Verified touch points:
- src/cli/commands/run.ts: runRunCommand (~:42). Add an additive `--team <id>` option; resolve via loadTeam(config, id) and thread the team id into runPipeline (the opts/teamId path added in Sprint 3). Absent flag => programming default (unchanged behavior).
- src/cli/commands/chat.ts: resolveRoleProviders at :28 and the ChatSession construction. Accept a team (positional arg like `bober chat <team>` per Phase 1, or --team) and pass the active team's namespace/providers into ChatSession so buildMemoryDistill (Sprint 2) and RunSpawner route to that team. Default 'programming'.
- Example team: declare it as a `teams` entry. For tests use a config fixture; optionally add a commented example to docs. Do NOT hardcode it as a code branch — it must flow through loadTeam (the whole point).
- Docs: add a section (README.md and/or docs/) titled e.g. 'Teams: adding a team is data, not code' covering the `teams` config shape ({displayName, memoryNamespace, pipelineShape, providers}), the three differentiation axes (provider routing / memory namespace / pipeline shape), the built-in programming default, and the deferred .bober/teams/*.json file registry. Cross-reference the research doc Phase 4.

Keep --team strictly additive (mirror Phase 1's --run-id). Tests: assert flag parse + propagation with an injected/stubbed runPipeline; assert chat resolves the named team; assert a lesson written during an active example-team session lands under .bober/memory/<example-namespace>/. Use temp dirs, no network.
evaluatorNotes:
sc-4-4: loadTeam(config,'example') returns the declared namespace/shape/providers (merged over resolved defaults).
sc-4-5: parse `run --team example` and assert the team id reaches runPipeline (stub it); parse `run` with no --team and assert programming default reaches runPipeline.
sc-4-6: construct the chat command with team 'example' and assert ChatSession uses the example namespace for buildMemoryDistill; with no team, programming/current path.
sc-4-7: drive a memory write through the active example team and assert the file lands under .bober/memory/<example-namespace>/, not .bober/memory/.
sc-4-8: confirm a docs file/section exists describing the teams config shape, the three axes, and the deferred file registry.
Regression: `bober run` with no --team unchanged; full suite green. Run npm run build, npm run typecheck, npm run test.
